attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a 12-hour history of vaginal spotting and pelvic pressure at 22 weeks gestation. This is her first pregnancy which has been uncomplicated until now. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins. She has no fever, abdominal pain, or vaginal discharge.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Doppler ultrasound reveals a reassuring fetal heart rate at 145bpm. Bedside ultrasound reveals cephalic presentation. Speculum examination reveals a cervix dilated to 3-4 cm with visible membranes at the external os. There is minimal spotting and no pooling of liquor. High vaginal swabs are collected.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Intravenous antibiotics, neonatal steroids and emergency cervical cerclage
Intravenous antibiotics and emergency cervical cerclage
Digital examination to assess for labour
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with painless cervical dilatation at 22 weeks gestation, without evidence of infection or active labour. This presentation is consistent with cervical insufficiency, which carries a high risk of pregnancy loss if untreated.

Intravenous antibiotics and emergency cervical cerclage is the most appropriate management. Emergency cerclage can improve pregnancy outcomes when performed between 16-24 weeks in carefully selected cases. Prophylactic antibiotics are given to reduce the risk of ascending infection during the procedure.

Intravenous antibiotics, neonatal steroids and emergency cervical cerclage would be inappropriate as antenatal corticosteroids are not indicated at 22 weeks gestation, which is below the current threshold for administration.

Expectant management with bed rest alone has not been shown to improve outcomes in cervical insufficiency. Without intervention, the risk of pregnancy loss is very high given the degree of cervical dilatation.

Digital examination should be avoided as it may increase the risk of infection or membrane rupture. The cervical dilatation has already been documented on speculum examination.

Tocolysis alone would not address the underlying problem of cervical insufficiency. While tocolysis might be considered alongside cerclage if there are contractions, it is not the primary management strategy.

After cerclage placement, the patient will require ongoing monitoring for complications such as infection, membrane rupture, or preterm labour. Success rates vary but can be as high as 70-80% in appropriately selected cases.
learningPoints: Emergency cervical cerclage may be offered between 16-24 weeks gestation when cervical dilatation occurs in the absence of labour or infection.
Prophylactic antibiotics should be given prior to emergency cervical cerclage to reduce the risk of chorioamnionitis.
Careful patient selection for emergency cerclage is crucial, with contraindications including active labour, infection, or significant bleeding.
references: Chatzakis C, Efthymiou A, Sotiriadis A, Makrydimas G. Emergency cerclage in singleton pregnancies with painless cervical dilatation: A meta-analysis. Acta Obstet Gynecol Scand. 2020 Nov;99(11):1444-1457. doi: 10.1111/aogs.13968. Epub 2020 Sep 16. PMID: 32757297.
National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). Preterm labour and birth [Internet]. London: NICE; 2015 [cited 2024 June 7]. Available from: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng25

Queensland Health. Guideline for premature rupture of membranes at term [Internet]. Brisbane: Queensland Health; 2021 [cited 2024 June 7]. Available from: https://www.health.qld.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0019/140149/g-ptl.pdf

Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Measurement of cervical length for prediction of preterm birth [Internet]. Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2023 [cited 2024 June 7]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Measurement-Cervical-Length-Preterm-Birth.pdf
